behaviors.description: Invalid signature requests are rejected with 401; body is not forwarded to queue
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is a deterministic local function (HMAC-SHA256 comparison). Mock the queue to verify it's never called; use real crypto.verify to catch bugs in comparison logic or raw-body handling. No I/O or external systems required.
behaviors.test_description: Test with both matching and non-matching signatures. Use actual HTTP request body format to ensure the handler computes the HMAC over the correct bytes. Assert 401 status and verify queue.enqueue was not called.

behaviors.description: Event is written to persistent queue before HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned
behaviors.justification: Verifying *ordering* of async operations requires testing actual queuing behavior, not just mocking. A mock queue that returns instantly could hide the bug 'forgot to await queue.enqueue()'. An in-memory queue lets you verify the event is actually in the queue before the response handler completes.
behaviors.test_description: Send a request and verify the event exists in the queue before the HTTP response completes. Use an in-memory queue implementation that provides actual persistence semantics (not just return-immediately mocks).

behaviors.description: Handler returns HTTP 200 without waiting for downstream event processing to complete
behaviors.justification: Testing async decoupling requires a downstream processor that can actually block. A mock processor returning instantly would pass even if the handler incorrectly awaits it. Integration test with a slow/mock processor reveals whether the response is truly asynchronous.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue an event with a slow downstream processor (e.g., 500ms delay). Measure time from request to HTTP 200 response. Verify response completes in <100ms, before processor finishes. Use real async/await, not mocked timers.

behaviors.description: Response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent
behaviors.justification: Requires concurrent request handling and real timing under load. Integration test with concurrent simulators and a slow processor can verify responsiveness. However, actual bottlenecks may only manifest with a real persistent queue under realistic concurrency.
behaviors.test_description: Send N concurrent requests while previous events are being processed slowly (e.g., 2 second handlers). Measure wall-clock time for each request's response. Verify all complete within 3 seconds. Use real async concurrency, not sequential mocking.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: May miss lock contention or latency issues in the real persistent queue system under actual concurrent load. If System-level testing reveals timeouts, escalate to test with real queue infrastructure.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to queue a second time
behaviors.justification: Deduplication requires persistent state (database, cache, or distributed store). The critical failure is a race condition: check-if-seen and enqueue are two separate operations. An in-memory dedup store lets you test the handler's logic, but atomicity issues may only appear under real concurrency.
behaviors.test_description: Send the same event ID twice, either sequentially (to test basic dedup) and concurrently (to stress atomicity). Use an in-memory dedup store (e.g., in-process Map or SQLite). Assert only one event appears in the queue. For race condition coverage, spawn concurrent requests simultaneously.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Race condition between dedup check and enqueue may only manifest under real HTTP concurrency or with a real distributed dedup store. If sequential tests pass but production sees duplicates, escalate to System-level testing with real concurrency.

behaviors.description: When queue reports it is full, handler returns 503 with Retry-After header
behaviors.justification: Error handling must be tested with a queue that can actually report fullness. A mocked error might not match real behavior (different error type, missing fields). Integration test with a queue that enforces a capacity limit verifies the handler catches and transforms the error correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Use a mock or test queue with a configurable capacity limit. Fill it, then attempt another enqueue. Verify handler returns 503 status. Assert Retry-After header is present and contains a reasonable value (e.g., 60 seconds).

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it
behaviors.justification: Testing queue capacity enforcement requires a real (or realistic) persistent queue, not a mock. Use a local instance (Docker PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.) with actual capacity constraints. This verifies the queue system itself, which is a component of the overall webhook handler system.
behaviors.test_description: Create a queue with capacity=N (e.g., 100 events). Enqueue N events; all should succeed. Attempt to enqueue the N+1 event; it should fail with a capacity-exceeded error. Verify the queue rejects, not silently drops or overwrites.
